questions.question: What is d_model in the Transformer base model?
references: d_model = 512
The Transformer base model uses d_model = 512.

questions.question: How many attention heads are used in the Transformer base model?
references: 8 attention heads
The Transformer base model uses 8 attention heads.

questions.question: How many encoder and decoder layers are used in the base Transformer?
references: 6 encoder layers and 6 decoder layers
6 layers in the encoder and 6 layers in the decoder
The base Transformer uses 6 encoder layers and 6 decoder layers.

questions.question: What value is used for label smoothing epsilon in Transformer training?
references: epsilon = 0.1
Transformer training uses label smoothing with epsilon = 0.1.

questions.question: What percentage of tokens are selected for masking in BERT pre-training?
references: 15 percent
BERT masks 15% of input tokens during pre-training.

questions.question: What are the two BERT pre-training objectives?
references: masked language modeling and next sentence prediction
MLM and NSP
BERT uses masked language modeling and next sentence prediction.

questions.question: How many parameters does BERT Large have?
references: 340 million
340 million parameters
BERT Large has about 340 million parameters.

references: SQuAD v1.1
the SQuAD v1.1 benchmark
BERT reported a 93.2 test F1 on the SQuAD v1.1 benchmark.

questions.question: What does LoRA keep fixed during adaptation?
references: the pre-trained model weights
LoRA freezes the pre-trained model weights
LoRA freezes the pre-trained model weights during adaptation.

questions.question: What kind of trainable parameters does LoRA add?
references: low-rank decomposition matrices
trainable low-rank matrices
LoRA adds trainable low-rank decomposition matrices to adaptation layers.

questions.question: Why does the Transformer reduce dependence on recurrence?
references: The Transformer replaces recurrent computation with self-attention, enabling parallel processing and more efficient long-range dependency modeling.
By relying on attention instead of recurrence, the Transformer improves parallelization while still capturing sequence dependencies.

questions.question: How do positional encodings help the Transformer?
references: Positional encodings inject order information into token representations so attention-based layers can use sequence position.
Because self-attention is permutation-invariant, positional encodings provide the model with information about token positions.

questions.question: Why is scaled dot-product attention divided by sqrt(d_k)?
references: The dot products are scaled by sqrt(d_k) to prevent large magnitudes that push softmax into saturated regions and hurt gradients.
Scaling by sqrt(d_k) stabilizes attention scores and training when key/query dimensionality is large.

questions.question: How does BERT differ from unidirectional language models?
references: BERT learns deeply bidirectional representations by conditioning on both left and right context simultaneously.
Unlike unidirectional language models, BERT uses masked language modeling to pre-train bidirectional context.

questions.question: Why does BERT use masking instead of standard left-to-right language modeling?
references: Masking enables BERT to use bidirectional context during pre-training rather than restricting context to one direction.
Masked language modeling lets BERT predict masked tokens from both sides, supporting bidirectional representations.

questions.question: What problem does next sentence prediction address in BERT?
references: Next sentence prediction helps BERT model inter-sentence relationships useful for tasks like question answering and natural language inference.
NSP trains BERT to understand whether two segments follow each other, improving sentence-pair reasoning.

questions.question: Why is LoRA considered parameter-efficient compared with full fine-tuning?
references: LoRA is parameter-efficient because it freezes original model weights and learns only small low-rank update matrices.
Instead of updating all parameters, LoRA trains low-rank adapters, greatly reducing trainable parameter count.

questions.question: How does LoRA avoid adding inference latency?
references: LoRA can merge low-rank updates into the base weights at inference, avoiding extra latency.
Because LoRA updates are low-rank and mergeable, it does not require additional inference-time computation overhead.

questions.question: What practical benefit does LoRA provide for deployment at scale?
references: LoRA allows many task-specific adapters to share one frozen base model, reducing storage and deployment overhead.
By training compact low-rank adapters, LoRA makes multi-task adaptation cheaper to store and serve.

questions.question: How are BERT and LoRA complementary in modern NLP workflows?
references: BERT provides strong pre-trained bidirectional representations, while LoRA provides a lightweight way to adapt such models to new tasks.
BERT is a pre-training architecture and LoRA is an adaptation technique, so LoRA can fine-tune BERT-like models efficiently.

questions.question: Does the Transformer core architecture rely on recurrence?
questions.gold_answer: No, the Transformer core architecture does not rely on recurrence.

questions.question: Does BERT pre-training use bidirectional context?
questions.gold_answer: Yes, BERT pre-training uses bidirectional context.

questions.question: Does LoRA fine-tune all original model parameters?
questions.gold_answer: No, LoRA does not fine-tune all original model parameters.

questions.question: Does LoRA claim reduced trainable parameters compared with full fine-tuning?
questions.gold_answer: Yes, LoRA claims reduced trainable parameters compared with full fine-tuning.
